Scaling Mental Health Interventions in Conflict Zones with Implementation Science

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Theresa Betancourt
3mo Edited
  • Report this post

Such an honor to be a part of this amazing BMJ Series on the Mental Health of Children Affected by Armed Conflict with such wonderful colleagues! Read our paper here: https://lnkd.in/eXuJ-Bq9 And see the full collection here: https://lnkd.in/eDDNrM8x May the years ahead mark a paradigm shift in moving beyond RCTs/trials to use the tools of implementation science to test strategies for scaling and sustaining with quality the many evidence-based MHPSS interventions that have been developed and evaluated in a range of cultures and contexts in the past 30 years. By putting forth and pursuing research questions under the auspices of implementation science (i.e. testing optimal strategies for training and supervision of nonspecialist MHPSS providers, integration of evidence-based MHPSS into existing systems, methods for increasing buy in and localization of evidence-based services, policy approaches to increasing budgetary and workforce investments in MHPSS) we can make these shifts as a field. Not only will a paradigm shift help us achieve greater success in the translation of our scientific progress in global MHPSS, but working with local partners we can strengthen and sustain local systems of MHPSS and social services in war-affected settings globally. According to the most recent data from UNICEF and Save the Children, approximately 520 million children now live in active conflict zones--we have no time to waste!

Peter Ventevogel
3mo Edited

Implementation and systems strengthening are urgently needed to integrate mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS) across health, education, and social protection platforms to reach children and families affected by conflict. In a new paper in The BMJ, my colleagues Serhat Yildirim, María Alejandra Gutiérrez-Torres, William Byansi, Tania Bosqui, Theresa Betancourt and I argue that scaling evidence based MHPSS for children and families in fragile and conflict affected settings is feasible when embedded within existing systems. Randomised trialsmay show effectiveness, but real-life implementation often is hampered by short funding cycles, workforce shortages, supervision gaps, fragmented sectors, and weak measurement of service quality. Progress requires phased integration of minimum evidence based intervention packages, such as those outlined in the IASC Minimum Service Package for MHPSS, into platforms including primary care, schools, and social protection, supported by supervision, referral pathways, and quality assurance. Learning partnerships among implementers, researchers, ministries, and communities using routine data can improve delivery, ensure equity, and monitor implementation quality and child outcomes. Focusing on these delivery questions can help ensure MHPSS becomes locally owned, scalable, and sustainable rather than short term. See the paper here: https://lnkd.in/erdxfAc7 This is part of a collection of papers that can be found here https://lnkd.in/ey3KEtR8. Thanks to World Innovation Summit for Health (WISH) for supporting this work. Inka Weissbecker, Julian Eaton, Richard Hurley, Jocalyn Clark, Zeinab Hijazi, PsyD, Ananda Galappatti, Marcio Gagliato, Allen G. K. Maina, Relinde Reiffers, Frederique Vallieres, PhD, Hivine Ali, Sofia Casas, Lynn Karanja, Sharon Rose Ataget, Felicity Brown, Naser Morina, PhD, Michael Pluess, Catherine Panter-Brick

Child mental health in conflict settings bmj.com

  • Dr. Michelle Nicole Chapa MD, MPH - Global Mental Health
    2mo
    • Report this post

    Last week, I was part of a conversation that left me thinking long after it ended. At the Know Your Policy (KYP-Lab) – March Edition hosted by Women in Public Health Leadership for Africa (WiPHLA), one thing became impossible to ignore… We looked at mental health policies from Nigeria and Kenya. The visions were clear. The strategies were there.The intentions were solid. And yet… something felt familiar. Not because we don’t know what to do. But because we are still not doing it at the scale and speed we should. One idea kept coming up again and again: Mental Health does not begin in psychiatric hospitals. It begins where people already are. Primary Health Care centers. Communities. Homes. When mental health is integrated into PHCs, something powerful happens: ✅ Care becomes accessible ✅ Stigma reduces ✅ Early intervention becomes possible And when non-specialist health workers are trained, when community health workers do home visits, when referrals are clear… we stop treating mental health as a distant, specialized issue. We bring it closer to everyday life. Then we looked at Cameroon. A system with limited psychiatrists. Limited facilities. High stigma. And yet… progress. Why? Because faith-based organizations and NGOs stepped in. Because communities became part of the solution. Because mental health support moved beyond hospital walls. It was a reminder that systems don’t always change from the top first. Sometimes, they shift because people decide to act where they are. That’s when it clicked for me. We often think: “If only there was more funding…” “If only the government moved faster…” “If only policies were better…” But the truth is… we already have frameworks. we already have models that work. we already have proof from countries like Nigeria, Kenya, and Cameroon. What we are missing is not knowledge. It’s coordinated action. And maybe even more importantly… it’s a shift in how we see our role. Because mental health is not just for psychiatrists. It belongs to: ✅ Teachers ✅ Employers ✅ Parents ✅ Religious leaders ✅ Media professionals ✅ Community leaders It belongs to all of us. Tradition, faith, and medicine don’t have to compete. They can coexist… if we are intentional. Yes, attitudes are changing. Families are starting to speak. The media is beginning to engage. But awareness alone is not enough. People need to believe that mental health conditions are real. That seeking help is not weakness. And that support can exist within their own communities. The gap between policy and practice is not a mystery. We know what needs to be done. What we need now are more people who: ✅ Understand the policies ✅ Are willing to collaborate across sectors ✅ And refuse to work in silos That’s is what WiPHLA is about. If you’re not yet part of this community, this is your invitation…because real change might not start in policy, but with us, working together differently. #PolicyToPractice #PublicHealthAfrica #WomenInLeadership

    Recently, the Government responded to the Health and Social Care Committee’s inquiry on community mental health services. We gave evidence last year - showcasing the strength of the mental health charity sector. It’s encouraging to see several of our priorities reflected in the Government's response: ✅Embedding lived experience in service design and delivery. ✅Moving away from short-term, competitive funding cycles towards longer-term funding through ICSs and local authorities. ✅Tackling inequalities in mental health services, including through the Patient and Carer Race Equality Framework (PCREF). Moving forward, we want to see: 💡Sustained Government commitment to the 24/7 neighbourhood mental health centre model, planning scale-up beyond the pilot programme. 💡Improved use of data to support the journey of patients through community mental health: our Mental Health Data Observatory provides the first national data infrastructure focused on VCFSE mental health provision, mapping community services, gaps in provision, and population need. https://lnkd.in/eseVZp8H #MentalHealth #VCFSE #Policy

    A huge step forward for young people’s mental health in Toronto—and a powerful signal of where the sector is heading. The recent $40M investment in the Labatt Family Thriving Minds Program is exactly the kind of bold, systems-level thinking we need right now. This isn’t just funding. It’s a shift toward integrated, connected care—bringing together SickKids, CAMH, and community-based services to create a more seamless experience for young people and their families. Because the reality is this: ➡️ Up to 1 in 5 young people in Canada experience mental illness ➡️ Only a fraction receive the care they need And too often, they face fragmented systems, long waitlists, and missed opportunities for early intervention. What’s exciting about this approach is the focus on:    •   Earlier access to care    •   Stronger coordination between hospital and community    •   Models shaped by youth voice and lived experience    •   Reducing barriers before crisis escalates This is exactly the kind of ecosystem thinking that organizations like Jack.org are proud to be part of. Because no single organization can solve this alone. Real progress happens when:    •   Clinical care    •   Community support    •   Peer-to-peer engagement    •   Prevention and early intervention …are all working together. At Jack.org, we see every day the power of equipping young people—and the adults around them—with the skills to recognize distress early and have meaningful conversations before things escalate. This investment strengthens the broader system we all rely on. More collaboration. More integration. More focus on prevention. That’s how we change outcomes. 👏 Congratulations to everyone involved in making this happen—and to the partners across Toronto continuing to push this work forward. #MentalHealth #YouthMentalHealth #Prevention #EarlyIntervention #SystemsChange #Jackorg https://lnkd.in/epMhgUWc

    It’s a threat… Some movements are loud. Some are silent. And some look harmless until you understand what they are disrupting. The Psychosocial First Aid and Recovery for Adolescent Ecosystems Conference (PSFAR-AE) is a threat. Yes, a threat. A threat to every agenda that benefits from: * Emotionally broken teenagers * Addicted minds * Policy complacency * Schools overwhelmed and unequipped * Parents disconnected * Gatekeepers asleep PSFAR-AE is dangerous to systems that thrive on silence and stigma because it is building strategy, not sympathy. It is a movement with structured backing, ecosystem collaboration, data-informed conversations, and practical psychosocial tools designed to: 🧶Strengthen adolescent mental resilien ✍🏻Equip educators beyond academics 😎Empower parents as first responders 👓Push policymakers beyond lip service 🤷🏼♀️Bridge faith, school, and community spaces This is not another “awareness” gathering. It is a coordinated response. When you bring together educators, psychosocial practitioners, youth advocates, school leaders, and policy influencers in one room, you are not hosting a conference—you are interrupting a cycle. And interruption feels like a threat to complacency. If you believe: * Teen wellbeing is a national asset * Prevention is smarter than rehabilitation * Ecosystems shape outcomes * Silence is no longer an option Then this movement concerns you. So the real question is not ✍🏻 what is PSFAR-AE about?* The real question is: 📌How will you be part of it? Will you: 🌱Register as a participant? 🧣Partner as an institution? ❤️🩹Sponsor strategic conversations? 🙌Mobilize educators in your network? 🤷🏼♀️Advocate policies that sustain adolescent psychosocial recovery frameworks? Movements that protect teenagers protect the future. And the future does not defend itself. How will you show up?

    📢 Rethinking Child Mental Health in Conflict Settings Today, I’m honoured to have led a BMJ editorial on Child and Caregiver Mental Health in Conflict-Affected Settings, alongside exceptional colleagues Ms. Faith Agumya, a suicide prevention advocate, Prof. Pallab Maulik, and Prof Abiodun Adewuya. Over 1 in 5 children globally now live in conflict-affected environments. The scale is unprecedented. But the response remains fragmented. If we are serious about protecting children’s mental health, we must shift the paradigm. 🔎 First, child recovery cannot be separated from caregiver wellbeing. Supporting parents’ mental health, stability, and livelihoods is not optional — it is foundational. 🏥 Second, we must move beyond short-term, stand-alone projects. Mental health care must be embedded within routine health, school, and social protection systems. 🧠 Third, the future of global mental health lies in understanding mechanisms of change. It is no longer enough to ask, “Does this intervention reduce symptoms?” We must ask: → How does it work? → For whom? → Under what conditions? → Can it sustain impact within real-world systems? 🌍 Fourth, scaling carefully is more powerful than scaling quickly. Task-sharing models and nationally integrated approaches, such as Group Support Psychotherapy in post-conflict Uganda, show that durable recovery is possible when interventions are locally led and system-embedded. The next frontier is clear: ✔ Align humanitarian and development financing ✔ Strengthen national leadership and coordination ✔ Invest in caregivers as a pathway to child wellbeing ✔ Build mental health systems that outlast crises Conflict may disrupt childhood — but with the right strategy, it does not have to define it. Read the Editorial herehttps://lnkd.in/d3xX4F4t #LSHTM #SEEKGSP #BMJ #ChildMentalHealth #GlobalMentalHealth #ConflictSettings #HealthSystemsStrengthening #ImplementationScience #MentalHealthLeadership

    Dr Antonis Kousoulis MD, DrPH

    Director - Global Mental Health Action Network

    3mo Edited

    We asked over 100 women's health organisations from around the world to tell us how we can improve mental health for women everywhere. This is what they told us... They said that women's mental health is a cross-cutting issue driven by poverty, violence, and inequality. The policy solutions, then, will not only come from the Health Minister. There are plenty of specific ideas on how to approach this in: - Social Protection (e.g. linking cash transfers to mental health outcomes); - Gender-Based Violence responses (e.g. mandating trauma-informed care in safe houses); - Education (e.g. compulsory mental health literacy); - Law Enforcement (e.g. anti-stigma training and diversion from the criminal justice system). They told us that the current healthcare system's reliance on centralised, specialist hospitals is making mental health services inaccessible, expensive, and stigmatised. Basic psychosocial support should be available in schools, faith-based groups, and women's groups. They said that stigma is often a greater barrier to care than cost. How to address this? - more anti-stigma campaigns, - more mental health literacy in schools, - more community-based prevention (e.g. peer-support circles and safe spaces), - more peer supporters and community health workers from key populations (e.g. people who use drugs, sex workers) as a part of the health workforce, - more gender-responsive protocols (e.g. trauma-informed care pathways for GBV survivors, mental health support integrated into maternal and adolescent health services) to address the distinct mental health needs of women and girls. Women's health organisations can help countries and systems to move towards strategies which ensure that funding and priorities reflect actual local community needs. Women form the vast majority of the global mental health workforce. Will we listen to them? https://lnkd.in/emMMwcEx #globalmentalhealth #womenshealth #womensmentalhealth #womenswellbeing #IWD2026 #GiveToGain #InternationalWomensDay Global Mental Health Action Network
